# Jeremiah 28: The Cost of False Comfort

Hananiah stands in the temple and speaks with confidence. His message is what Jerusalem desperately wants to hear: the yoke of Babylon is broken, the temple treasures are coming home, the exiles will return—all within two years. When he seizes the wooden yoke from Jeremiah's neck and snaps it, the gesture is powerful and symbolic. Here is a man apparently speaking for God, declaring liberation. And Jeremiah's initial response—"Amen, may the LORD do so"—reveals something crucial: Jeremiah himself longs for this outcome. He is not opposed to good news. He simply knows the difference between what we wish were true and what actually is. When Jeremiah appeals to the pattern of the prophets who came before, he is not being clever or defensive. He is pointing to a hard truth: true prophets typically announce judgment and calamity, not comfort. A prophet who speaks only pleasant words earns credibility only if those words come to pass.

But Hananiah's breaking of the wooden yoke is not the end of the conversation—it is merely the beginning of Jeremiah's prophetic response. The word of the LORD comes to Jeremiah afterward with devastating clarity: Hananiah has replaced a wooden yoke with one of iron. The symbolic gesture meant to declare freedom actually prophesies something far worse. God Himself has placed an iron yoke on all the nations, and they will serve Nebuchadnezzar. This is not softened or left ambiguous. Hananiah has not simply made an honest mistake; he has persuaded the people to trust in a lie, and his rebellion against the LORD will cost him his life. He dies within months, his prophetic word proven false and his voice silenced.

What makes this chapter so unsettling is that it shows us the seductive power of false prophecy. Hananiah was not obviously evil or clearly demon-possessed when he spoke. He used the language of faith, he stood in the house of God, and he offered a message of hope. The people would naturally have been drawn to him over Jeremiah, whose message was consistently dark and demanding. Yet the test of a true prophet is not comfort or eloquence but the correspondence between his words and God's actual will. God was indeed bringing Babylon's judgment, and no amount of fervent speech could change that reality. The tragedy is that the people of Judah would suffer not only from Babylon's conquest but also from having believed a lie that prevented them from repenting and preparing their hearts for what was to come.

This chapter confronts us with a question that cuts deeper than historical interest: How do we distinguish between what we want to be true and what God is actually saying? False prophets do not announce obviously wicked things; they announce pleasant things falsely. They tell us that our sin will not have consequences, that repentance is unnecessary, that God's patience is infinite and requires nothing of us. Their words appeal to our desires, and therein lies their power—and their danger. The only safeguard against such deception is a deepening commitment to God's Word itself, a willingness to accept hard truth when Scripture speaks it, and the humility to ask whether our spiritual comfort might sometimes obscure our spiritual need.
